title:
The Sacred Naga in Khmer Culture: Serpentine Symbol of Protection, Pow
– HD Asian Art
highlights: Found across temple balustrades, lintels, pediments, and ceremonial stairways, the Naga is not merely decorative—it is a guardian of sacred space, a protector of divine order, and a symbol of royal legitimacy and cosmic balance.  ...    Naga became a bridge between heaven, earth, and the watery realms of the underworld—a role reflected in architecture,  ...    Nowhere is the importance of the Naga more visible than in Khmer temple architecture, particularly during the Angkor period (9th–15th centuries), when monumental temples like Angkor Wat, Bayon, and Baphuon were constructed as cosmic microcosms. Naga balustrades, typically with five or seven heads, line temple causeways and stairways. These multi-headed serpents are often shown flaring dramatically at the ends, their bodies stretching like a coiling guardrail along sacred paths.
